evidence.title: Evaluation of Acute Abdominal Pain in Adults | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Computed tomography (CT) with intravenous contrast media is recommended for evaluating adults with acute right lower quadrant pain; CT with oral and intravenous contrast media is recommended for patients with left lower quadrant pain. 15 , 16 Sigmoid diverticulitis is the most common cause of left lower quadrant pain in adults, and CT has a reported sensitivity of 79 to 99 percent for detecting the condition. 15 CT is better than ultrasonography for diagnosing appendicitis and can detect extracolonic causes of abdominal pain.

evidence.title: Evaluation of Acute Pelvic Pain in Women | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Urinalysis of a midstream urine specimen should be considered to assess for urinary tract infection. Urine culture is often beneficial to confirm suspected urinary tract infection, identify specific organisms, and guide antibiotic use. If testing for gonorrhea and chlamydia, first-void urine collected without cleaning of the urethra (“dirty” catch) has historically been recommended, often requiring two urine samples for full evaluation. 26 Logistically this can be challenging. One study found that a midstream sample for chlamydia testing is still 96% accurate, and another study found a sensitivity of 86.2% for chlamydia and 94.4% for gonorrhea. This shows noninferiority to a dirty catch; therefore, one urine sample may be sufficient. 26 , 27

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old adult presenting with a 2-day history of lower left-sided abdominal pain. The pain is associated with fever, chills, and a general feeling of being unwell. There is some nausea but no vomiting, no changes in bowel movements, and no urinary symptoms. The pain worsens with movement, coughing, or pressure on the lower abdomen. There is no history of similar episodes or known bowel conditions. No vaginal symptoms or changes in appetite or weight are reported.

O: Phone encounter only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute diverticulitis given the localized left lower quadrant pain with systemic symptoms such as fever and chills.
Differential:
1. Acute diverticulitis
2. Left-sided urinary tract infection or pyelonephritis
3. Colitis or infectious gastroenteritis
4. Gynecologic pathology (less likely given no vaginal symptoms and no pregnancy)

P: - First-line medication: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily plus Metronidazole 500 mg orally three times daily for 7–10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 160/800 mg orally twice daily plus Metronidazole 500 mg orally three times daily for 7–10 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: Avoid penicillin due to allergy; monitor for gastrointestinal side effects and signs of worsening infection; other contraindications not assessed in this telephone encounter.
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if abdominal pain worsens, fever persists or increases, or if new symptoms such as vomiting, inability to tolerate oral intake, or signs of sepsis develop. Seek emergency care if severe abdominal pain or signs of peritonitis occur.
- Testing: Recommend in-person evaluation including abdominal imaging (CT scan) to confirm diagnosis and assess for complications; laboratory studies including CBC and inflammatory markers as clinically indicated.
